title: Responsible AI Use
description: Learn the principles of using AI responsibly — from respecting privacy to avoiding harmful applications to understanding the environmental impact of AI.
learningObjectives: Apply core principles of responsible AI use including privacy awareness, harm avoidance, and transparency
Evaluate what personal and sensitive information is appropriate to share with AI systems
Navigate academic integrity guidelines when using AI for learning and coursework

sections.title: Principles of Responsible AI Use
sections.content:
Using AI responsibly is not about following a rigid rulebook — it is about developing a set of principles that guide your decisions as AI becomes a bigger part of daily life. These principles apply whether you are a student, a professional, or someone who uses AI casually.

**Transparency: be honest about AI involvement.** When you use AI to help with work, be upfront about it with the people who rely on that work. If Claude helped you write a report, your team lead should know. If AI generated part of a presentation, mention it. Transparency builds trust and helps others evaluate the output appropriately. Hiding AI use does not just risk your credibility — it can mislead others into thinking the work represents something it does not.

**Accountability: you are responsible for the output.** Even though Claude generated the text, you are responsible for what you do with it. If you publish an AI-generated article with factual errors, you cannot blame the AI — you chose to publish it without adequate review. This principle is simple but important: when you use AI output, you own it. Review it, verify it, and stand behind it.

**Proportionality: match AI use to the situation.** Using Claude to draft a casual email is perfectly fine. Using Claude to make a medical diagnosis for yourself without consulting a doctor is risky and irresponsible. The appropriateness of AI use depends on the context, the stakes, and whether you have the expertise to evaluate the output. Ask yourself: "Am I using AI as a tool to enhance my capabilities, or as a substitute for expertise I do not have?"

**Do no harm: consider the downstream effects.** Before using AI for a task, think about who might be affected by the output and how. Could AI-generated content mislead someone? Could it be used to manipulate or deceive? Could it reinforce harmful stereotypes? Responsible AI use means thinking beyond your immediate task to consider the broader impact of what you produce.

sections.title: Privacy and Data Considerations
sections.content:
What you share with AI systems matters. Understanding how your data is handled and what is safe to share helps you protect both yourself and others.

**What happens to your conversations with Claude.** When you chat with Claude, your messages are processed to generate responses. Depending on the platform and settings, conversations may be stored temporarily or used to improve the model. Different products have different privacy policies — Anthropic's API and consumer products have different data handling practices. If privacy is important for a particular conversation, read the terms of service for the specific product you are using.

**Information you should be cautious about sharing.** Be thoughtful about sharing:

• **Personal identification data** — Social Security numbers, driver's license numbers, passport numbers, financial account numbers. There is rarely a good reason to share these with AI.
• **Other people's private information** — Sharing someone else's personal details, private messages, or confidential information without their consent raises ethical issues regardless of how the AI handles the data.
• **Confidential business information** — Trade secrets, unreleased product plans, internal strategy documents, or proprietary data. If your employer has policies about AI use, follow them.
• **Sensitive personal matters** — While Claude can discuss sensitive topics thoughtfully, remember that these conversations may be stored. Consider whether you would be comfortable if the conversation were seen by someone else.

**Practical guidelines for data safety.** When possible, anonymize sensitive information before sharing it with Claude. Instead of "My employee John Smith at 123 Main Street has a performance issue," try "An employee in my team is underperforming. Here is the situation..." You get the same quality of advice without exposing personal details. When working with business data, check your organization's AI use policy before pasting in proprietary information.

**The evolving landscape.** AI privacy practices are rapidly evolving, with new regulations and standards emerging regularly. Stay informed about the privacy policies of the AI tools you use, and do not assume that today's practices will remain the same tomorrow. When in doubt, err on the side of sharing less rather than more.

sections.title: Avoiding Harmful Applications
sections.content:
AI tools like Claude are designed with safety measures, but as a user, you also have a responsibility to use AI in ways that do not cause harm.

**Misinformation and deception.** One of the most significant risks of AI is its ability to generate convincing but false content. Using Claude to deliberately create misinformation — fake news articles, fabricated evidence, deceptive reviews, or fraudulent communications — is a clear misuse. Even unintentionally, sharing unverified AI-generated content as fact can spread misinformation. Always review and verify before sharing.

**Impersonation and manipulation.** Using AI to impersonate real people — mimicking their writing style to send messages as them, creating fake social media posts attributed to them, or generating content that falsely represents their views — is harmful and in many cases illegal. Similarly, using AI to craft manipulative messages designed to exploit vulnerable people crosses an ethical line.

**Avoiding bias amplification.** If you ask Claude to help with tasks like writing job descriptions, evaluating candidates, or assessing performance, be aware that AI may inadvertently reproduce biases from its training data. A job description generated by AI might unconsciously favor certain demographics or use language that discourages diverse applicants. Review AI-assisted outputs with an eye for fairness and inclusivity.

**Respecting AI safety boundaries.** Claude has built-in safety measures that prevent it from helping with certain harmful tasks. When Claude declines a request, it is not being difficult — these boundaries exist to prevent real harm. Attempting to circumvent safety measures is both irresponsible and usually unsuccessful with well-designed AI systems.

**A simple test.** Before using AI for any task, ask yourself: "Would I be comfortable if everyone knew exactly how I used AI here?" If the answer is no, reconsider your approach. Responsible AI use should withstand public scrutiny.

sections.title: AI and Academic Integrity
sections.content:
If you are a student, or if you are involved in any learning or certification context, the question of how to use AI ethically is especially important. The rules are evolving, and getting this right matters for both your integrity and your learning.

**The core principle: AI should enhance your learning, not replace it.** The purpose of assignments, essays, and exams is to develop and demonstrate your understanding. If Claude writes your essay for you, you might get a grade, but you will not develop the thinking and writing skills the assignment was designed to build. Using AI to skip the learning process ultimately hurts you the most.

**What most institutions consider acceptable.** While policies vary widely, most educational institutions are comfortable with:

• Using AI to understand concepts you are struggling with (like a tutor)
• Getting help brainstorming or outlining ideas (as a thinking partner)
• Checking your grammar and writing mechanics (like a spell checker)
• Practicing with AI-generated quiz questions (as a study tool)

**What most institutions consider problematic.** Generally, these cross the line:

• Submitting AI-generated text as your own original work
• Having AI complete assignments, problem sets, or take-home exams
• Using AI during closed-book tests or exams that prohibit outside tools
• Copying AI explanations without understanding the underlying concepts

**The gray area.** Many uses fall somewhere in between, and this is where institutional policies and instructor guidance matter. Is it okay to have Claude help you revise a draft? Can you use AI-generated outlines? What about using Claude to help debug code in a programming course? These answers depend on your specific institution and instructor. When in doubt, ask.

**Best practices for academic AI use.** Always check your institution's and your instructor's policies on AI use. When you do use AI as a study aid, document how you used it — many instructors appreciate transparency. Make sure you can explain and defend any work you submit, because if you cannot explain what you wrote, it is a sign that AI did too much of the thinking for you.

sections.title: Identify Ethical Considerations in AI Use Cases
sections.content: Practice recognizing the ethical dimensions of real-world AI use cases.

exercise.prompt:
For each of the following scenarios, identify the ethical considerations at play and recommend whether the AI use is appropriate, inappropriate, or needs modification. Explain your reasoning for each.

**Scenario 1:** A manager uses Claude to write performance reviews for their team members, copying the output directly into the company's HR system without editing.

**Scenario 2:** A student uses Claude to explain a difficult calculus concept they are struggling with, asks practice questions, and then solves their homework problems themselves.

**Scenario 3:** A job applicant uses Claude to rewrite their resume and cover letter, tailoring them to a specific job posting. They review and edit the output to ensure it accurately represents their experience.

**Scenario 4:** A person shares their friend's private text messages with Claude and asks for advice on how to respond in a way that will manipulate the friend into lending them money.

**Scenario 5:** A small business owner uses Claude to generate 50 fake five-star reviews for their restaurant on a review website.

For each scenario, address: (1) What principles are at play? (2) Who could be harmed and how? (3) Your recommendation and reasoning.
hints: Think about the transparency principle — in which scenarios is the AI use being hidden from people who should know about it?
Consider the accountability principle — who is responsible for the output in each case, and are they taking that responsibility seriously?
For the student scenario, think about whether the AI use enhances or replaces the learning process
Look for scenarios where other people's rights or well-being are affected by the AI use
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should analyze all five scenarios with specific ethical reasoning. Scenario 1 should be identified as problematic — performance reviews have real consequences for employees, the manager is not reviewing the output, and the employees do not know AI was used for something so personal and impactful. Scenario 2 should be identified as appropriate — the student is using AI as a tutor and still doing the work themselves, which enhances rather than replaces learning. Scenario 3 should be identified as generally appropriate — the applicant reviews and edits the output, ensures accuracy, and the resume still represents their real experience. Scenario 4 should be identified as clearly inappropriate — it involves sharing someone's private messages without consent AND using AI to craft manipulative communication. Scenario 5 should be identified as clearly inappropriate and likely illegal — it involves deliberate deception, harms consumers and competing businesses, and violates review platform terms of service. Each analysis should reference relevant principles (transparency, accountability, do no harm, proportionality) and identify specific potential harms.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Reflect on the principles of responsible AI use.

exercise.prompt: A colleague tells you: "I just use Claude for everything at work now — writing emails, creating reports, drafting proposals. I never tell anyone because the output is good enough that nobody can tell." What would you say to this colleague about responsible AI use, and which specific principles are they potentially violating?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should identify at least two principles being violated: transparency (hiding AI use from colleagues and stakeholders who may be making decisions based on this work) and accountability (not reviewing output carefully enough to stand behind it). A strong answer will also note the proportionality concern — using AI for everything without distinction between appropriate and inappropriate use cases. The response should be constructive rather than judgmental, acknowledging that AI use is valid while emphasizing the importance of being transparent with colleagues, reviewing output carefully, and considering whether each specific use case is appropriate. Bonus for mentioning that some of the colleague's work products may have real consequences for others who deserve to know how they were produced.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Here is what you should remember about using AI responsibly:

• **Be transparent about AI use.** When AI contributed to your work, let the relevant people know. Transparency builds trust and helps others evaluate the output appropriately.

• **You own the output.** Regardless of what AI generated, you are responsible for how you use it. Review everything, verify important claims, and be prepared to stand behind the work.

• **Protect privacy — yours and others'.** Be cautious about what you share with AI, especially personal identification data, confidential business information, and other people's private information. When possible, anonymize sensitive details.

• **Consider the downstream effects.** Before using AI for a task, think about who could be affected and how. Avoid applications that could spread misinformation, deceive others, amplify bias, or cause harm.

• **Use AI to enhance learning, not replace it.** If you are a student, use AI as a study partner that helps you understand and practice, not as a shortcut that does the thinking for you. Check your institution's policies and be transparent with instructors.
